item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this article — the whole conspiracy thing about forgetting everything about surgery is pretty cool, if that's what it's supposed to be. Although I am confused about one thing: why is this in the Ad Astra canon? To me this has nothing to do with it. I'm wrong, obviously, because it's on the canon page, but I'd like to know. Edit: I get it now, I just had to use my big brain.

Also as a sidenote I am interested in writing for the ad astra canon, specifically early timeline.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Never knew this was part of a larger narrative, this article is possibly one of my favorite SCP items on the wiki for how it completely changes how one looks at the average normalcy of the world it inhabits. Absolutely wonderful concept. +1-ed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
reminder that apostrophes never show plural!

~the apostrophe knight

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bit of a necropost, but I want to share some of my own feelings regarding these things.

The final collapsible is absolutely unrealistic and unbelievable… in the context of the real world. But this article takes place in the Foundationverse, a world where anomalous things are established to take place. The Foundation alone contains thousands of physics breaking things, which would point to the fact that there is more to the world than our understanding of physics and nature.

I also feel that many of these points are addressed by the article itself. The fact that this knowledge could have been forgotten naturally is addressed as implausible, which in turn implies intentional action. And is it that unbelievable to think that there is a global conspiracy, in a world where one already exists. The article even establishes that the Foundation has done similar things in the past.

Personally, I feel like the final collapsible has quite a bit of intrigue and flows quite a lot better than just an exposition dump.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Double this, the spoiler ruined for me a perfect story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first part of the article was great. Chilling body horror, good pacing and building just the right atmosphere. It was exellent. And I really don't want to be that guy,

but
but

The moment I opened the spoiler was like diving head first into an "Ancient Aliens" episode. Same arguments, same figures of speech, etc. But lets start with what it actually tells us and how.

It's clear that this part of the article is centered around the assumption that the process described in SCP-3484 is, in fact, completely non-anomalous. After a brief statement that the text in the book does not contain a memetic infection (which is hinted pretty clearly in the main part of the article) the main part starts with a peculiar statement:

But the effect must be anomalous!

For sure it must! Because such process clearly contradicts most laws of biology, anatomy and thermodynamics. And this question is even raised again after a few paragraphs. The explanation for such claims should be very interesting, but, sadly, it doesn't exist. Instead the author through the mouths of some O5 starts to somehow connect normality of the process with the claim that it is "forgotten knowledge of the ancients".

Thats where the "ancient technologies" stuff kicks in. All the classics - the mythical lost secrets of Damascus steel (that in historical perspective were equivalent to losing your granny's cake recipe. It's just one of many types of steel, not even that good by what we know now), the bowless Tasmanians (which slowly regressed without contact with other world during dozen thousands of years), etc, that can be summarized as "maybe something like that could probably happen, why not".

The fact that no method can appear in fully complete form out of thin air and if it really was known in past, there should be other instances of it or it's predecessors is totally ignored, instead focusing on the claim that "if you are so smart, why aren't you rich if it's that good, why weren't it popular everywhere". This one is actually pretty simple, if we remember that 150 years ago there were no online book databases and so a hundred or so books about wacky Psychic surgery-esk techniques could easily fade into oblivion in libraries.

Instead the ardicle delves into the new depths of theories, suggesting that the whole humanity was lobotomized using this method (and carrying this change to the next generations despite Charles Darwin rotating in his grave like a jet turbine) either to delete the memory of this exact process, which somehow stayed in this exact brain part (which is a kick to the groin to the whole neurobiology surprisingly acknowledged in the next paragraph) or for some other unknown reason. And, as a cherry on top, next goes a statement that suggests a massive conspiracy behind all that as an ultimate explanation. Thats just… wow.

But hey logic ain't always the whole point of the work. Maybe we should crank suspension of disbelief up to 11 and just enjoy how its written. Well…

First and foremost its a pure, unfiltered exposition dump bigger than rest of the article. Furthermore, the text adds generally nothing not told in the main part of the article untill the revelation of the book's page 87 content and the accompanying theory (which it immediately proves nonsensical, but guess what, admitting something is nonsensical does not add any sence there). The whole thing can be summarised in a single "what if its a nonanomalous method that we just forgotten" proposal with volume of two paragraphs tops. 90% of spoiler's content can be cut out without any loss in the narrative. And I'm not even touching on what it does to the pacing and tone of the article.

All in all it was a huge upvote from me untill the spoiler. Now its a definite -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just realized today while discussing skips with someone that this is super-related to [[SCP-3716]]; so related that it's imaginable a crosslink might be a good idea.

It's easy to imagine that the 'trick' the skeleton-monks learned to unzipping their skin was derived from a copy (or excerpt) from this book.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I quite liked this!

On one hand, I'd have preferred it if certain implications (like the fact that the book was printed, implying the existence of others) been left implicit rather than explicit, allowing them to emerge as fridge logic (I'd have particularly liked it if it had done this with the implication of a part of the brain we all chose to remove). In short, part of me wishes the O5 note wasn't there.

On the other hand? A lot of the fridge logic that the note elaborates on is stuff 80% of your readers would probably have missed. Making it an explicit part of the text was likely the right call; it also helps that the article is fairly short.

Explicit or not, this was a solid +1. It reminds me of a much more modern and solid version of SCP-291.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn! I really wish I’d read this before nominating my favourites of 2018. This is standout - a beautifully balanced article with horror, intrigue and humour. Someone else nominate this, quick!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Easily one of my favorite SCPs. +1 with enthusiasm

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a student.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Do you teach a novel writing class or are you in one?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Incidentally, I recently read this SCP to my novel writing class, and they had fun with it. No one had known about the SCP Foundation before. I might start using this as my go-to introductory article for others.

(They were intrigued when I offered the warning that the article involves body horror, but without injury.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, that's disturbing.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This makes me deeply uncomfortable, both for reasons I can explain and for some I cannot. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The brain part kinda reminded me of SCP-2828, although im an idiot so maybe they are completely different.

+1 for antimemetic knowledge fields and detachable Knees!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like it, like it alot, your not going after the dooms-day with a bang: your going after it with a book. A nice contrast to having just read though the terrifying SCP-3984. +1

Edit: You clever son-of-a-bitch, you tied in the Prion-Spiders, didn't you. I swear to god if this story ends with the Prion-Spiders saving the world i will up-vote the entire thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great. The O5 discussion was especially well done.

I'm curious how this ties in to doomsday, I can't think of anything right away. Absolutely looking forward to the other entries.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty much agree with Peppers here. Amazing story here, and it definitely sets the stage. Amazing as expected, WJS.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the excellent. Great anomaly that sets the stage for further shenanigans while telling a complete story on its own. +1

I'm guessing LETHE is the tie-in with your doomsday?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really love this. The body horror is very effective, if subtle.
I know it's just a new style, and it has its merit, but these huge sprawling meta-super narrative scp's are getting a little repetitive, and it's nice to see short and sweet skips like this are coming back into style.

Big +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Augenlappens out for SCP-3484

I'll admit, it took me until one of the O5s pointed out that there was no memetic effect for this to really stick with me. But the story is told in the little details around the edges, and everything that's left out. It's pretty impressive!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That note from R. Argent genuinely had my recoil in my seat the first time I read it. This is some wonderfully creepy stuff and a good hook for an overarching narrative.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this a lot, and the O5 Council discussion is great (though I agree with CWW that the text colours aren't immediately easily distinguishable).
I didn't get body horror from it though - it just seems fun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great. I love the in-document discussion about this anomaly; It's very similar to your 01 proposal in style, which I love as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The text colors aren't quite vibrant enough to be easily distinguishable, but it's still a very cool anomaly with an intriguing backstory. Happily upvoted, can't wait to see what y'all have in store.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really, really like this. The body horror is excellent, if a little too reminiscent of Neal Shusterman's Unwind, and I adore the way you use your 001 to introduce the main narrative thrust of your team's entries.

item.title: Author's Post
item.content:encoded:
Here is the first chapter in the Doomsday Contest team with Cyantreuse, Rimple, and LordStonefish.

The image is from page 193 of Rauber's Lehrbuch der Anatomie des Menschen, published in 1904 and now in the public domain.

Many thanks to my team, and the crit of 9Volt, Sly161, and Fantem.
